items.title: One Patch to Caption Them All: A Unified Zero-Shot Captioning Framework
items.alt: One Patch to Caption Them All: A Unified Zero-Shot Captioning Framework
items.authors: Lorenzo Bianchi*, Giacomo Pacini*, Fabio Carrara, Nicola Messina, Giuseppe Amato, Fabrizio Falchi
items.venue: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2026
description: Traditional zero-shot captioners generate text from global image features, limiting their ability to describe specific regions or fine details. We introduce Patch-ioner, a unified framework that shifts from an image-centric to a patch-centric paradigm, enabling flexible captioning of arbitrary regions without region-level supervision.

In Patch-ioner, each image patch is treated as an independent captioning unit. By aggregating patch representations, we can produce captions for any area — from small regions to entire images. Leveraging DINO-based backbones with strong localized representations, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results in several zero-shot captioning tasks, including dense, region-set, and the newly introduced trace captioning.
This patch-level formulation unifies local and global captioning within a single framework, offering scalable and flexible zero-shot caption generation without paired image-text data.

items.title: Reviving ConvNeXt for Efficient Convolutional Diffusion Models
items.alt: Reviving ConvNeXt for Efficient Convolutional Diffusion Models
items.authors: Taesung Kwon, Lorenzo Bianchi, Lennart Wittke, Felix Watine, Fabio Carrara, Jong Chul Ye, Romann M. Weber, Vinicius C. Azevedo
items.venue: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2026
description: Recent diffusion models increasingly rely on Transformer backbones for scalability, but often at the cost of high computational demands.
In this work, we introduce the Fully Convolutional Diffusion Model (FCDM), a ConvNeXt-inspired backbone redesigned specifically for conditional diffusion modeling.

FCDM integrates adaptive conditioning into ConvNeXt blocks and organizes them within an easily scalable U-shaped architecture parameterized by only two hyperparameters. Despite being fully convolutional, FCDM matches or surpasses Transformer-based diffusion models while requiring approximately 50% fewer FLOPs and delivering substantial throughput improvements.

On ImageNet at 256×256 and 512×512 resolution, FCDM-XL achieves state-of-the-art efficiency, faster convergence, and competitive FID performance compared to Diffusion Transformers. Our results demonstrate that modern convolutional architectures remain a powerful and highly efficient alternative for scaling diffusion models.

items.title: Talking to DINO: Bridging Self-Supervised Vision Backbones with Language for Open-Vocabulary Segmentation
items.alt: Talking to DINO: Bridging Self-Supervised Vision Backbones with Language for Open-Vocabulary Segmentation
items.authors: Luca Barsellotti*, Lorenzo Bianchi*, Nicola Messina, Fabio Carrara, Marcella Cornia, Lorenzo Baraldi, Fabrizio Falchi, Rita Cucchiara
items.venue: IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), 2025
description: While CLIP has shown remarkable vision-language understanding at the global image level, it lacks fine-grained understanding. On the other hand, DINOv2 exhibits strong semantic correspondences at the patch level, but as a self-supervised backbone, it isn’t natively aligned with text.

In this paper, we propose to bridge these two models by learning a mapping from CLIP text embeddings into the feature space of DINOv2, enabling open-vocabulary segmentation. This mapping is learned through a contrastive training that exploits the self-attention heads of DINOv2, which are able to highlight homogeneous regions of the image, to align a caption only with its corresponding region.

items.title: The devil is in the fine-grained details: Evaluating open-vocabulary object detectors for fine-grained understanding
items.alt: The devil is in the fine-grained details: Evaluating open-vocabulary object detectors for fine-grained understanding
items.authors: Lorenzo Bianchi, Fabio Carrara, Nicola Messina, Claudio Gennaro, Fabrizio Falchi
items.venue: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) (Highlight Paper), 2024
description: Recent advances in open-vocabulary object detection (OVD) allow models to recognize objects described in natural language, yet they remain limited in understanding fine-grained details like color, texture, or material.

This paper introduces Fine-Grained Open-Vocabulary Detection (FG-OVD) — a new benchmark suite and evaluation protocol designed to test how well detectors can differentiate subtle object attributes. Using large language models (LLMs), we generate rich captions describing object properties, along with negative variants that swap attributes to probe model sensitivity.

Experiments reveal that while current state-of-the-art OVD models perform well on standard benchmarks, they often fail to recognize fine attribute distinctions. FG-OVD provides the first comprehensive framework to analyze and improve detectors’ fine-grained visual-language understanding.

items.title: CountingDINO: A Training-free Pipeline for Class-Agnostic Counting using Unsupervised Backbones
items.alt: CountingDINO: A Training-free Pipeline for Class-Agnostic Counting using Unsupervised Backbones
items.authors: Giacomo Pacini*, Lorenzo Bianchi*, Luca Ciampi, Nicola Messina, Giuseppe Amato, Fabrizio Falchi
items.venue: IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV), 2026
description: Class-agnostic counting (CAC) aims to estimate object counts without being restricted to predefined categories. We introduce CountingDINO, the first training-free, exemplar-based CAC framework that leverages self-supervised DINO features to extract object-aware representations without any labeled data.

At inference, exemplar features are extracted from user-specified regions and used to generate similarity maps, which are normalized into density maps for object counting. This approach scales to images of varying complexity and object size by partitioning and aggregating feature maps.

Evaluated on the FSC-147 benchmark, CountingDINO outperforms training-free baselines, remains competitive with supervised methods, and demonstrates that effective, label-free CAC is possible for large-scale, flexible applications.

items.title: ReCoptic: Computer Vision for the Reconstruction of Dismembered Coptic Codices
items.alt: ReCoptic: Computer Vision for the Reconstruction of Dismembered Coptic Codices
items.authors: Lorenzo Bianchi, Alejandro Moreo, Fabrizio Falchi, Fabrizio Sebastiani, Costanza Bianchi
items.venue: IEEE International Conference on Cyber Humanities (IEEE CH) (Best Paper Award), 2025
description: Many Coptic manuscripts have been dismembered over the centuries, their pages scattered across libraries and collections worldwide. Reuniting these fragments is crucial for understanding the culture of Coptic-speaking communities but remains a highly challenging and time-consuming task.

We introduce ReCoptic, a probabilistic image-based system that helps scholars virtually reconstruct ancient Coptic manuscripts. By analyzing scans of individual pages, ReCoptic estimates the probability that two pages come from the same manuscript and ranks all possible pairs accordingly.

Trained through supervised machine learning on labeled page pairs, ReCoptic autonomously captures visual features such as line spacing, handwriting, and character size. Tested on over 6,000 manuscript pages, it achieves high accuracy and offers an efficient, data-driven way to accelerate manuscript reconstruction.

items.title: Is CLIP the main roadblock for fine-grained open-world perception?
items.alt: Is CLIP the main roadblock for fine-grained open-world perception?
items.authors: Lorenzo Bianchi, Fabio Carrara, Nicola Messina, Fabrizio Falchi
items.venue: International Conference on Content-Based Multimedia Indexing (CBMI) (Best Paper Award), 2024
description: Open-vocabulary object detection (OVD) enables models to recognize objects described by free-form text, allowing adaptation to new concepts beyond the training set. While CLIP and similar vision-language backbones drive this progress, they often struggle with fine-grained recognition—distinguishing subtle visual attributes such as color, shape, or material.

We investigate these limitations by evaluating CLIP on a fine-grained object-matching benchmark and find that its latent space poorly separates detailed object characteristics. Our analysis shows that fine-grained cues are indeed present in CLIP embeddings but are not fully exploited by standard similarity metrics like cosine matching.

By adding simple re-projection layers on frozen CLIP encoders, we demonstrate improved discrimination of fine-grained attributes, opening the path toward more perceptually detailed vision-language backbones.
